metadata.title: Tracing the dynamics of misinformation and vaccine stance in Finland amid COVID-19

abstract: This study analyzed 1,683,700 vaccine-related tweets in Finnish using FinBERT language model, Botometer, and BERTopic, from December 2019 to October 2022. A strong correlation was identified between Negative Stance towards Vaccines (NSV) and misinformation, with an upward trend over time and a significant role of malicious bot accounts. Topic modeling revealed persistent themes of vaccine skepticism and diverse misinformation themes adapting to debunking efforts. Approximately a third of tweets contained misinformation, irrespective of source reliability, leading to increased NSV. The study observed a gap in active countering of misinformation by authorities, suggesting proactive involvement by public authorities, public education for effective misinformation management and social media literacy.

sections.paper_id: Unlu et al. - 2025 - Tracing the dynamics of misinformation and vaccine stance in Finland amid COVID-19
paragraphs: The COVID-19 pandemic has significantly impacted global public health, posing major challenges such as misinformation and vaccine hesitancy (WHO, 2021). In communication, frameworks like strategic, risk, crisis, and health communication are vital for managing public health emergencies and conveying information about hazards, threats, and health matters (Coombs, 2012;Fischhoff, 1995;Kreps, 2011;Stieglitz et al., 2018). These frameworks emphasize the importance of human and digital dimensions in healthcare and well-being for effective communication strategies. The integration of social media into daily life has altered information access (Tian & Robinson, 2021), underscoring the need for strategic communication in risk and crisis contexts to foster trust among vaccine-hesitant individuals (Hudson & Montelpare, 2021).

However, these interconnected communication concepts also face challenges due to social media's potential to hinder effective health communication (Kreuter & Wray, 2003;Suarez-Lledo & Alvarez-Galvez, 2022). Recent research has revealed that online discussions are influenced by a variety of dynamics. For example, large coordinated networks involved in political astroturfing (Jemielniak & Krempovych, 2021), along with social media bots, tend to engage more frequently with conspiracy theories related to COVID-19 (Weng & Lin, 2022;Xu & Sasahara, 2022). Similarly, factors such as political polarization, political trust, media preferences, exposure to politicians, digital media, and personal contacts are associated with an increased propensity for conspiracy and misinformation beliefs (De Coninck et al., 2021;Suter et al., 2022).

These research studied the intricate relationship between online discourse and public health outcomes during the pandemic, such as nature of infodemic (Cinelli et al., 2020), public discourse and top concerns on social media (Abd-Alrazaq et al., 2020;Jang et al., 2021), global sentiments (Lwin et al., 2020), the impact of bots on political conspiracies and sentiments manipulation (Ferrara, 2020;Shi et al., 2020), as well as their impact on vaccine uptake, adherence to health guidelines, public trust in institutions, and information credibility (Chipidza, 2021;X. A. Zhang & Cozma, 2022).

As a result, addressing public health emergencies like the COVID-19 pandemic requires a comprehensive understanding of risk, crisis, and health communication to shape health-related behaviors, influence public health policies, and improve health outcomes (WHO, 2018).

In the scope of this study, we employed the Negative Stance towards Vaccines (NSV) as an indicator for vaccine hesitancy. NSV has emerged by several factors including but not limited to misinformation, rushed development of vaccines, government trust, doubts about the efficiency of the vaccine, its adverse effects and personal rights (Peterson et al., 2022;Rosenkilde et al., 2023;Troiano & Nardi, 2021).

Social media platforms, such as Twitter (new X), can function as amplifiers for misinformation and NSV with the capacity to distort online public opinion and manipulate sentiment (Wang et al., 2022). Literature shows that individuals exposed to and endorsing misinformation and NSV tended to exhibit reduced adherence to social distancing measures, compliance with official guidelines, and support for COVID-19 public policies (Tangcharoensathien et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2022). Moreover, a consistent correlation was identified between belief in misinformation and a lack of intention to receive a vaccine (Bruns et al., 2022;Loomba et al., 2021;Pierri et al., 2022).

However, it is essential to recognize that, in Finland, vaccination is not mandatory, nor is it unlawful to express skepticism or negative opinions about vaccines or the vaccination process. Nevertheless, the occurrence of NSV, coupled with the spread of misinformation and their interplay, necessitates careful monitoring, particularly considering their potential impact on vaccine uptake.

The potential of social media to exacerbate NSV poses significant challenges for public health authorities striving to disseminate accurate information in such complex environments (Basu & Dutta, 2008). Analyzing these contexts, such as the discourse around vaccine stances on Twitter, can enhance our understanding of these challenges and inform effective strategies (Street & Haidet, 2011).

Therefore, to effectively counteract misinformation and NSV, adaptive and responsive health communication strategies are crucial. These involve regular monitoring of online public discourse (Purnat et al., 2021), acknowledging the importance of receiver feedback (Rimal & Lapinski, 2009) and tailoring messages to accommodate health literacy, cultural beliefs, and individual factors (Andrulis & Brach, 2007).
These approaches are pivotal to navigating misinformation and ensuring communication is dynamic and relevant.

One approach to managing infodemic and understanding the drivers of risk perception involves social listening, a process that entails gathering user-generated, unfiltered (Pomputius, 2019) online and offline data regarding public perceptions (Neiger et al., 2013). Social listening enables the identification of emerging trends, user needs, and priorities, while keeping communicators informed of crises, misinformation, public sentiment, as well as cultural and demographic factors that may influence risk perception (Stieglitz et al., 2018). Additionally, it enables the assessment of adherence to prevention measures and prompt implementation of data-supported interventions in real-time (Merchant & Lurie, 2020;Reid & Duffy, 2018). Projects such as the Red Crescent COVID-19 dashboards and WHO Early Artificial Intelligence-Supported Response with social listening have been employed to monitor and manage infodemics (IFRC, 2023;WHO, 2023).

Social listening involves not only capturing and analyzing public social media discussions and campaign responses within societies, but also social media communications from organizations themselves (Lucas & Landman, 2021). This requires a proactive and attentive approach to monitoring, interpreting and responding various stimuli communicated through electronic, mediated, and social channels (Stewart & Arnold, 2018). This enables effective management of infodemic by facilitating the dissemination of information on risks, promoting behavioral changes, and encouraging public participation in decision-making regarding emergency measures (Glik, 2007).

To the best of our knowledge, no study has extensively explored the misinformation-NSV dynamic in Finnish context. Previous studies have employed various qualitative methods to investigate different aspects of risk communication, such as the types of risk perceptions (Lohiniva, Pensola, et al., 2022), the social listening skills of practitioners (Lohiniva, Sibenberg, et al., 2022), COVID-19-related stigma (Lohiniva et al., 2021), the role of social media influencers (Pöyry et al., 2022), the impact of information sources on preventive measures (Farooq et al., 2021), public contestation toward THL (Väliverronen et al., 2020), government strategy for managing crises (Tiirinki et al., 2022), the effect of pandemic on Finnish health system (Karreinen et al., 2023) and change in political mistrust (Unlu et al., 2023).

This longitudinal study is pivotal in understanding the connections among misinformation, NSV, social media bots, health agencies, and the Finnish population during COVID-19, via Twitter data. It's crucial for creating tailored health communication strategies to counter misinformation, build trust in health authorities, and boost vaccination rates. The research underscores social media's influence on public opinion during health crises.
To achieve our research objectives, we address the following research questions:

(1) How did misinformation and NSV start spreading during COVID-19, how did they change over time? (2) What were the main topics discussed in misinformation and NSV tweets, and how did they change during the time? (3) What are the common and different topics between misinformation and NSV?

While these methods provide deep understanding of public health perceptions, they have limitations, addressed subsequently.

sections.paper_id: Unlu et al. - 2025 - Tracing the dynamics of misinformation and vaccine stance in Finland amid COVID-19
paragraphs: The significant role of Twitter in health communication is marked by its high interaction volume through posts and comments (Cinelli et al., 2020). It's a key channel for sharing medical knowledge and facilitating dialogues between healthcare professionals and the public (Chan et al., 2020;Rosenberg et al., 2020). Our study demonstrates Twitter's efficacy in offering insights into the opinions of a specific demographic, despite its lack of representativeness of the general population. This platform's quick-paced conversational nature is particularly valuable for health communication researchers for rapidly assessing public health opinions (Lee et al., 2024).

For the data collection, we constructed 147 query keywords from a set of 14 Finnish terms 1 through conjugation, inflection, and phrasing. Utilizing the R package academict-witteR (Barrie & Ho, 2021), we extracted tweets containing any of the query keywords between 1st December 2019 and 24th October 2022. These search categories generated a total of 1,683,700 tweets from 60,560 unique Twitter accounts. Furthermore, the tweet data consist of 724,214 retweets, 57,865 quoted, and 901,621 original tweets.

sections.paper_id: Unlu et al. - 2025 - Tracing the dynamics of misinformation and vaccine stance in Finland amid COVID-19
paragraphs: The text classification aims to categorize the dataset according to vaccination stances and misinformation. Given that our dataset was unlabeled, we first created a labeled subset from the primary dataset. This labeled data was then used to fine-tune a pre-trained language model for classification tasks. Finally, the trained classifiers were applied to label the remaining dataset.

In this study, misinformation is defined as any false, inaccurate, or conspiratorial information pertaining to COVID-19 and vaccination. Our research was not designed to make distinctions between misinformation and disinformation or between various types of misinformation, including sarcasm, fraudulent treatments/cures, panic buying, and conspiracy theories. Our annotation training adhered to the COVID-19 misinformation codebook by Memon and Carley (2020), a model for subsequent studies (Moffitt et al., 2021). Additionally, to stay current with evolving COVID-19 information, we consulted fact-checking sites and the Finnish Institutions for Health and Welfare webpage. For the purposes of the research design, tweets were categorized into one of two categories depending on whether the content of the tweet contained or related to misinformation.

To address vaccine hesitancy, we utilized a methodology previously established for stance detection (Du et al., 2017) and later adapted for the COVID-19 context (Lindelöf et al., 2022;Medford et al., 2020). According to this code book, a tweet can take a positive, negative, or neutral/unclear stance on the COVID vaccines. For our research, we condensed these categories after annotation into binary classifications, with a score of 1 for a NSV and a score of 0 for a positive or neutral/unclear position.

In the initial stage of assembling the training data, four native Finnish research assistants manually labeled 4150 texts for both categories. The training data were randomly chosen from the primary dataset, adhering to the following inclusion criteria: the tweet must be written in Finnish, not be a retweet, and exceed 30 characters in length. We iteratively trained the annotators in six sessions and evaluated the inter-rater reliability using Krippendorff's alpha coefficient for each session. On average, the Krippendorff's alpha for stance detection and misinformation was 0.693 and 0.668, respectively. Both results are considered good inter-rater reliability. 2 From 4150 tweets, the manual annotation labels 1118 (0.27%) tweets with NSV and 709 (0.17%) tweets containing misinformation.

We utilized BERT, a prominent Transformer-based pre-trained language model, widely applied in text classification tasks (Devlin et al., 2018;Minaee et al., 2022). BERT can be fine-tuned for various languages. For Finnish language, the FinBERT pre-trained model (Virtanen et al., 2019) outperforms other state-of-the-art models (Hämäläinen et al., 2021;Jahan et al., 2022). Figure 1 illustrates the fine-tuning parameters and results with FinBERT.

To generate robust results, we meticulously explored several text classification algorithms including logistic regression, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, and FinBERT, with the comparative analysis detailed in Table 1 in supplementary file. Fin-BERT emerged as the superior model for our specific dataset.

Following this, we embarked on an optimization phase focused on FinBERT, examining various parameters such as the inclusion or exclusion of URLs and hashtags, as well as the application of lemmatization. This rigorous process aimed to enhance the performance of our chosen model, thus driving our research towards achieving optimal accuracy.

As displayed in Figure 1, the annotated data was cleaned by removing URL links, emojis, and unnecessary symbols (e.g., #,, RT). The pre-processed data is used to train Fin-BERT model. The output from FinBERT is passed to a softmax layer resulting in a probability value. If this probability value is higher than a threshold (default threshold is 0.5) (Galke et al., 2023), the classification label is 1 (e.g., misinformation). By implementing additional hyper-parameter tuning techniques, such as weighted loss and tuning threshold, the fine-tuned model for sentiment classification achieve an accuracy of 0.94, and a F1 score of 0.89 on the test dataset. The results for misinformation classification model are lower -0.85 for accuracy and 0.67 for F1 score.

sections.text:
The text classification aims to categorize the dataset according to vaccination stances and misinformation. Given that our dataset was unlabeled, we first created a labeled subset from the primary dataset. This labeled data was then used to fine-tune a pre-trained language model for classification tasks. Finally, the trained classifiers were applied to label the remaining dataset.

In this study, misinformation is defined as any false, inaccurate, or conspiratorial information pertaining to COVID-19 and vaccination. Our research was not designed to make distinctions between misinformation and disinformation or between various types of misinformation, including sarcasm, fraudulent treatments/cures, panic buying, and conspiracy theories. Our annotation training adhered to the COVID-19 misinformation codebook by Memon and Carley (2020), a model for subsequent studies (Moffitt et al., 2021). Additionally, to stay current with evolving COVID-19 information, we consulted fact-checking sites and the Finnish Institutions for Health and Welfare webpage. For the purposes of the research design, tweets were categorized into one of two categories depending on whether the content of the tweet contained or related to misinformation.

To address vaccine hesitancy, we utilized a methodology previously established for stance detection (Du et al., 2017) and later adapted for the COVID-19 context (Lindelöf et al., 2022;Medford et al., 2020). According to this code book, a tweet can take a positive, negative, or neutral/unclear stance on the COVID vaccines. For our research, we condensed these categories after annotation into binary classifications, with a score of 1 for a NSV and a score of 0 for a positive or neutral/unclear position.

In the initial stage of assembling the training data, four native Finnish research assistants manually labeled 4150 texts for both categories. The training data were randomly chosen from the primary dataset, adhering to the following inclusion criteria: the tweet must be written in Finnish, not be a retweet, and exceed 30 characters in length. We iteratively trained the annotators in six sessions and evaluated the inter-rater reliability using Krippendorff's alpha coefficient for each session. On average, the Krippendorff's alpha for stance detection and misinformation was 0.693 and 0.668, respectively. Both results are considered good inter-rater reliability. 2 From 4150 tweets, the manual annotation labels 1118 (0.27%) tweets with NSV and 709 (0.17%) tweets containing misinformation.

We utilized BERT, a prominent Transformer-based pre-trained language model, widely applied in text classification tasks (Devlin et al., 2018;Minaee et al., 2022). BERT can be fine-tuned for various languages. For Finnish language, the FinBERT pre-trained model (Virtanen et al., 2019) outperforms other state-of-the-art models (Hämäläinen et al., 2021;Jahan et al., 2022). Figure 1 illustrates the fine-tuning parameters and results with FinBERT.

To generate robust results, we meticulously explored several text classification algorithms including logistic regression, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, and FinBERT, with the comparative analysis detailed in Table 1 in supplementary file. Fin-BERT emerged as the superior model for our specific dataset.

Following this, we embarked on an optimization phase focused on FinBERT, examining various parameters such as the inclusion or exclusion of URLs and hashtags, as well as the application of lemmatization. This rigorous process aimed to enhance the performance of our chosen model, thus driving our research towards achieving optimal accuracy.

As displayed in Figure 1, the annotated data was cleaned by removing URL links, emojis, and unnecessary symbols (e.g., #,, RT). The pre-processed data is used to train Fin-BERT model. The output from FinBERT is passed to a softmax layer resulting in a probability value. If this probability value is higher than a threshold (default threshold is 0.5) (Galke et al., 2023), the classification label is 1 (e.g., misinformation). By implementing additional hyper-parameter tuning techniques, such as weighted loss and tuning threshold, the fine-tuned model for sentiment classification achieve an accuracy of 0.94, and a F1 score of 0.89 on the test dataset. The results for misinformation classification model are lower -0.85 for accuracy and 0.67 for F1 score.

The sentiment classification model indicates 446,400 tweets with NSV, which is almost 27% of our dataset. Similarly, the misinformation classification model identifies 460,087 tweets as misinformation, accounting for over 27% of the dataset.

paragraphs: To detect automated Twitter accounts in the study, we employ the fourth version of Botometer software to differentiate between bot-like and human-like accounts (Gilani et al., 2019;Yang et al., 2019). This version of Botometer incorporates a wider array of features and an advanced supervised machine learning methodology to improve its performance and respond to earlier critiques. It utilizes specialized classifiers tailored to each type of bot, aggregating these assessments to generate a comprehensive final score. Over 1,200 features from each Twitter account are utilized in the algorithm, encompassing account metadata and content information, as well as sentiment from the account's 200 most recent tweets (Sayyadiharikandeh et al., 2020). Botometer assigns a bot score between 0 and 1 for each account, and a Complete Automation Probability (CAP) score for a probabilistic interpretation of the bot score. To identify automated accounts, we adopt the Botometer suggested threshold of a CAP score of 0.804 or higher, equivalent to a bot score of 0.43 or higher (Yang et al., 2022). Furthermore, for users whose primary language is English, we utilized the default CAP score. Conversely, when dealing with Finnish speakers, we applied the universal score (Sayyadiharikandeh et al., 2020). Notably, our study contributes to the field by showcasing the application of Botometer in languages other than English.

Despite its widespread adoption and purported effectiveness in social science research, Botometer still exhibits noticeable flaws (Gallwitz & Kreil, 2022). Our manual checks revealed mislabeling of government, newspaper, and some politician accounts as bots. Consequently, we have undertaken further validation analyses and calculations to counteract and lessen the impact of these limitations (Rauchfleisch & Kaiser, 2020). Following the workflow guidelines proposed by Yang et al. (2022), we also assessed the efficacy of Botometer across different CAP-score thresholds, including our selected threshold. This assessment, performed on 100 manually annotated X-accounts, yielded an accuracy rate of 0.87. A comprehensive account of the validation methodology and outcomes is provided in the supplementary material.

Furthermore, to precisely identify bots related to COVID-19, we have refined the Botometer results with a custom classification function. This function includes extra features, penalties, and decision thresholds, specifically designed to detect what we refer to as malicious bots in the COVID-19 context. Features considered include misinformation and sentiment ratios, account status, the proportion of COVID-19-related tweets, and account age relative to Finland's first COVID-19 case. Upon assessing these features and imposing penalties, bot accounts with an aggregate penalty surpassing 0.5 are categorized as malicious. This classification refers to bot accounts explicitly established for disseminating misinformation or expressing NSV (see details in supplementary file).

paragraphs: BERTopic (Grootendorst, 2022) modeling clusters documents (tweets here) based on their semantic similarity between BERT embeddings (Devlin et al., 2018), which is utilized to extract dominant topics from NSV or misinformation tweets. Egger and Yu (2022) suggest that BERTopic is capable of not only providing distinctive topics, but also able to generate new insights comparing to other models by on-negative matrix factorization (NMF), Top2Vec and latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA).

Tweets embodying NSV and misinformation were extracted from our dataset and embedded using the FinBERT language model. Dimensionality was reduced using UMAP, followed by document clustering using HDBSCAN. Topic representations were then derived via the cTF-IDF algorithm. The BERTopic framework, with customizable parameters (Table 4 in supplementary file) for dimension reduction, embedding cluster methods, and topic representation, was employed throughout the process (Grootendorst, 2022).

Building on the topics identified through BERTopic, we will apply thematic content analysis to gain a nuanced understanding of the discussions contained within the tweets (Clarke & Hayfield, 2015). To ensure a broad and representative view of each topic, we selected the top 10 tweets per topic based on their c-TF-IDF scores. This selection process ensures we capture a wide range of stances and misinformation types within each topic. By closely analyzing these tweets, we explored the justifications and argumentations made, the distinct perspectives adopted, and how specific concepts were interpreted within these conversations. Thus, our approach moves beyond merely identifying the 'what' of the topics to understanding the 'how' and 'why' of their respective discourses on Twitter.

sections.paper_id: Unlu et al. - 2025 - Tracing the dynamics of misinformation and vaccine stance in Finland amid COVID-19
Unlu et al. - 2025 - Tracing the dynamics of misinformation and vaccine stance in Finland amid COVID-19
paragraphs: Regarding our initial research questions, our findings reveal a fluctuating trend in misinformation and NSV over time. We found a significant correlation of 0.991 between daily NSV and misinformation tweets, suggesting they often go hand in hand. This correlation increased marginally to 0.993 and 0.994 when focusing solely on tweets with NSV or those spreading misinformation, respectively. This result aligns with the finding from a meta-analysis study by Skafle et al. (2022), which found claims of a connection between misinformation on social media and COVID-19 vaccine hesitancy from 19 papers.

Furthermore, Figure 2 illustrates a steady increase in the ratio of tweets containing NSV to all tweets about COVID-19 vaccine (blue line). A similar trend is seen in the ratio of misinformation tweets to all vaccine-related tweets (orange line). Specifically, we observe an increase of almost 10% in both the NSV and misinformation ratios in the first year (2020-2021). In the second year of COVID-19 (2021COVID-19 ( -2022)), these ratios increased up to 20%, resulting in over 30% of tweets at the end of 2022 containing some form of NSV or misinformation. This trend slows down in the last year (2022-October 2022), with an increase of over 10%. The trend of NSV in Finnish Twitter between 2021 and 2022 is similar to the sentiment trend reported by Reshi et al. (2022) from a Worldwide Twitter Dataset.

With the monthly volume of tweets for each label, we identified three quantiles, which correspond to 0%-40% (Q1), 40%-80% (Q2), and 80%-100% (Q3) of the tweet volume distribution. This, in turn, allows us to define four distinct periods based on the monthly volume of tweets: low (December 2019-November 2020), developing (December 2020-July 2021), peak (August 2021-February 2022), and plateau (March 2022-October 2022). Figure 3 illustrates the NSV and misinformation tweet count timeline as well as the four periods.

paragraphs: Overall, the bot classification algorithm detects 13,522 unique bot accounts, which is equivalent to 22.3% of all accounts in our dataset. Within bot accounts, 4894 accounts are further classified as malicious (8% of all accounts). Figure 2 illustrates an opposite trend between the ratio of neutral bot tweets and malicious bot tweets. Specifically, while the ratio of neutral bot tweets to all COVID-19-related tweets (green dashed line) steadily reduced over the course of the pandemic, the ratio of malicious bot tweets (red dashed line) increased during the same period.

Regarding the role of bot accounts in spreading NSV and misinformation messages, neutral bot accounts have close to zero impact on the overall conversation as shown by the purple and gray lines from Figure 2. On the other hand, malicious bots played a rising role, albeit less than 1%, starting from July 2021 (pink and gray lines).

On the other hand, malicious bots played a rising role, albeit less than 1%, starting from July 2021 (pink and gray lines).

sections.paper_id: Unlu et al. - 2025 - Tracing the dynamics of misinformation and vaccine stance in Finland amid COVID-19
paragraphs: We distilled these themes to the top ten based on frequency within each category for a more focused analysis. Table 2 summarizes these ten most common themes in both anti-vaccine and misinformation tweets. Studying these prevalent narratives can shed light on the primary drivers of vaccine skepticism and misinformation, guiding targeted strategies for vaccine communication and misinformation debunking.

Table 1 highlights relevant topics discussed on Finnish Twitter. During the pandemic, certain topics emerged prominently in the public debates, especially in cases there were uncertainties until sufficient evidence still accumulating. Understandably, such topics were discussed actively also in social media and the bots quickly involved in these discussions.

These topics include skepticism about COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness (e.g., general vaccine topic, third wave of epidemic, Israel's rapid rollout of vaccinations before other countries, hospitalization due to COVID-19 and/or due to vaccinations, and the pandemic situation) and concerns about vaccine safety (e.g., vaccine side effects and media, myocarditis, and spike protein) were prominent themes. Geographical topics included Sputnik, Russia, and the EU vaccination passport. These topic rankings reflected Finland's context as a European country neighboring Russia. These topics overlapped with the misinformation dataset, except for death and preventing COVID-19's spread, which were unique. frequency. The peak frequency for most topics occurred around October to December 2021, followed by a gradual decrease in discussion frequency.

Another aspect of topic development is the keywords describing each topic. Table 2 displays keywords for the top 10 topics in both datasets, including unique topics from the misinformation data highlighted. By combining topic timelines and the development of topic keywords, we observed that keyword variations occurred primarily in the initial period, before 2021, after which they became more consistent.

The analysis reveals various insights into the topic development and keywords used in discussions related to COVID-19 vaccines on Finnish Twitter. The general vaccine topic experienced fluctuations, with early discussions focusing on conspiracy theories involving Bill Gates and GSK, a pharmaceutical company. Hesitancy towards the vaccine became a dominant theme from the end of 2020, peaking in December 2021. In this period, keywords associated with this topic included prevention, death, and Pfizer.

Another topic with high fluctuation in keywords is preventing the spread of COVID-19. Prior to October 2020, keywords associated with this topic include virus, immunity, recognize and symptoms; and the tweet contents discussed other vaccines (e.g., flu, HPV) and compared vaccine efficacy with the human immune system. Later, the narrative shifted to emphasizing prevention, protection, and the potential for vaccinated individuals to spread the infection. Similar patterns were observed in the pandemic topic, which initially denied the existence of the pandemic but later centered on the idea of a pandemic of the vaccinated.

The Sputnik topic remained consistent, primarily focusing on the Sputnik vaccine, Russia, and Putin, with a significant spike in discussions about Russia and new keywords like Ukraine and NATO in early 2022. Other consistent topics appear to be those about the safety and side-effects of COVID-19 vaccines. These are death, Israel, hospitalization, vaccine side-effects and media, myocarditis, and spike protein. Although first appeared at the beginning of 2020, these topics increased in frequency after December 2020. The keywords describing these topics are mainly consistent and mostly express concerns about vaccine safety (hospitalization/death due to side-effects, heart attack and myocarditis, poison protein in vaccines) and effectiveness (vaccinated get hospitalized, media censor of side-effects, vaccine-effect in Israel). Similarly, the topic of vaccine passports consistently dismissed their need due to perceived low vaccine effectiveness. Lastly, the keywords used for the topic 'third' appears to be consistent especially after September 2020. This topic group cover tweets mentioning three, third, second, fourth and so on. We observed several instances within this group where the word 'three' was used in combination with other words, examples of which include 'the three deaths,' 'three different vaccines,' 'third wave of COVID-19,' 'third stage of vaccine trial,' and 'three research papers.' From those, the most noticeable themes are concerns about vaccine effectiveness (e.g., third, fourth and fifth dosages/COVID-19 wave), safety (e.g., third death/hospitalization from side-effect).

sections.paper_id: Unlu et al. - 2025 - Tracing the dynamics of misinformation and vaccine stance in Finland amid COVID-19
sections.header_raw: Comparison of themes in misinformation and NSV topics
sections.header_norm: comparison of themes in misinformation and nsv topics
paragraphs: Addressing our final research question, Table 1 shows that misinformation and NSV data overlap in several topics. For the NSV data, the general vaccine topic contains multiple keywords that express skepticism about the effectiveness of the vaccine. Besides Bill Gates and GSK, we uncovered other narratives such as questioning the vaccine development timeline and the effectiveness of the vaccine (e.g., the 'almost ready' vaccine, illegal vaccine trials, not prevent spreading infection), undermining the need of taking vaccine (e.g., natural immunity is always better), claiming of side-effects (e.g., making men/ women sterile, causing brain damage, vaccine-related deaths and sickness), expressing the unwillingness to take vaccine (e.g., liberty and body autonomy) or demanding other not to take vaccines, and spreading conspiracy (e.g., 5G, micro-chip, aluminum in vaccine). Both death of vaccinated individuals and the prevention of COVID-19 infection are the most noticeable sub-topics from the start of 2021, after first dosage of vaccines got distributed in Finland.

On the other hand, we observed more specific and consistent topics about vaccine skepticism in the misinformation dataset. In this data, the two most dominant topics are death and preventing the spreading of COVID-19, both of which are combined within the general vaccine topic in NSV data.

Another unique topic within the NSV is about the evolving nature of pandemic. Prior to 2021, this topic comprised of tweets spreading conspiracy claims like questioning the existence of COVID-19 pandemic, claiming pandemic is over, solving root causes of the pandemic. After 2021, the representative tweets under this topic group expressed skepticism towards the effect of vaccine against infection by calling the number of infections within the vaccinated population a pandemic (e.g., the pandemic of the vaccinated, highest case count when vaccines are available).

paragraphs: BERTopic models have identified most representation topic groups, as well as how each topic evolved and changed over time. Using a codebook first developed by Kata (2010) and adapted by Zhao et al. (2023) for classification of vaccine misinformation, we divide the top 10 topics for both dataset into 7 themes: conspiracy, concerns on vaccine safety and efficacy, no need for vaccines, morality, liberty, humor, and overstatement. As a result of the fluidity of topic development, a topic group can communicate multiple themes. Table 3 provides an overview of the common themes found from top 10 topics of both NSV and misinformation data. Table 3 categorizes the topics and sub-topics found from both NSV and misinformation data in the Finnish context. Many of the themes we identified are also identified in previous studies, like the rush in vaccine development, a conspiracy of vaccine-profiting pharmaceutical companies and global elites, and similar claims of side effects and safety issues of the vaccine (Bruns et al., 2022;Lindelöf et al., 2022;Nadesan, 2022;Zhao et al., 2023). Furthermore, we identified some unique topics and examples, including Sputnik and Putin's vaccine, the conspiracy of Israel and Pfizer's CEO, and the sentiment against vaccination passports. Finally, we did not find any topics or sub-topics that can be classified under the humor theme.

sections.paper_id: Unlu et al. - 2025 - Tracing the dynamics of misinformation and vaccine stance in Finland amid COVID-19
paragraphs: Our study reveals that a substantial proportion of tweets circulating misinformation and NSV during the pandemic; roughly one in three tweets propagated false, deceitful, malicious information about vaccinations. User engagement, including shares and likes, with these problematic tweets did not differ significantly (Kouzy et al., 2020). This suggests that misinformation can easily disseminate on social media platforms, regardless of source reliability (Cinelli et al., 2020). Lindelöf et al. (2022) found that the number of NSV and conspiracy theories declined following the initial global English Twitter discourse on vaccine rollout. However, our findings suggest that, after this initial reduction, a significant spike was observed during the period of the third vaccine rollout and the discovery of the COVID-19 Omicron variant.

Our research uncovered a strong correlation between the spread of misinformation and NSV on Twitter, noting a particularly alarming rise in both factors throughout the pandemic. This linear relationship aligns with findings from Skafle et al. (2022) and is further evidenced by Loomba et al. (2021) in their randomized controlled trial, who found that online misinformation led to a decrease in vaccination intent by 6.2% in the UK and 6.4% in the USA. Pierri et al. (2022) also identified a negative correlation between misinformation and COVID-19 vaccination uptake rates, reinforcing the link between misinformation and NSV. Interestingly, the situation in Finland diverges from these trends. Despite the prevalence of NSV in online discussions, Finland's vaccination rates have remained high, suggesting that the vocal online NSV community does not reflect the wider population's views. This observation points towards the complexity of translating social media sentiment into actual behavioral outcomes, with Finland exemplifying how real-world actions can deviate from online discourse.

Our analysis indicates temporal fluctuations in the spread of misinformation and NSV, with periods of peak activity followed by stabilization. This pattern may suggest a saturation or normalization of these narratives within public discourse.

However, the challenge of drawing concrete conclusions from social media data cannot be understated, given the complexities of research design and data limitations. The dynamic nature of social media complicates the identification of clear causal relationships, necessitating a cautious approach to data interpretation and emphasizing the importance of rigorous methodology in understanding the impact of misinformation on public health decisions.

The role of bots in this discourse is noteworthy, especially given the increase of malicious bots over the course of the pandemic (Al-Rawi & Shukla, 2020;Suarez-Lledo & Alvarez-Galvez, 2022;Weng & Lin, 2022). Our study identified a significant number of bot accounts actively involved in disseminating misinformation and expressing NSV concerning COVID-19. However, these accounts were not the primary influencers in shaping the overall discourse on Twitter. Our independent bot analysis (Unlu et al., n.d.) reveals that the range of topics propagated by malicious bots is broad and varies in response to the pandemic's progression, mirroring overall trends. The key focus of these bots' messages revolves around safety, political/conspiracy theories, and choicerelated issues. They exhibit a pattern of critiquing pandemic containment strategies, expressing dissent towards political figures, and sowing skepticism about the veracity of information disseminated on social media.

Through BERTopic modeling, we uncovered the complex and evolving nature of vaccine discourse on Twitter during the COVID-19 pandemic. We observed a significant upsurge in NSV and misinformation discussions in the end of 2021, gradually tapering thereafter. This trend reflects the fluidity of public sentiment in response to evolving circumstances and information during a health crisis.

The discourse originally focused on the purported ties between Bill Gates and pharmaceutical company GSK, shifting later towards vaccine hesitancy. This trend began in late 2020 and peaked in December 2021, with notable keywords being prevention, death, and Pfizer, suggesting public sentiment was swayed by perceptions of the vaccine's safety and efficacy. Our findings suggest that groups propelled by conspiracy theories consistently introduced new agendas to public discourse, contributing to the persistence of such theories. In this stage, vaccinerelated misinformation was disseminated not only by anti-vaccine activists but also systematically by professional news sources across several countries (Jemielniak & Krempovych, 2021). However, while political ideology and reliance on conservative media can exacerbate the situation, vaccination intentions in the US context were discovered to have less correlated with political ideology (Romer & Jamieson, 2020). Furthermore, given that Finland has one of the highest levels of trust in news media and press readership in the EU (Ala-Fossi et al., 2023), incorporating mainstream media appears to be effective in promoting both preventive actions and vaccination uptake.

Our model also revealed that the key topics in tweets promoting misinformation mirrored those causing vaccine hesitancy, such as the death of vaccinated individuals, prevention of COVID infection, and vaccine effectiveness. This suggests that those spreading misinformation and those hesitant to vaccinate share similar concerns.

Through exploring the common themes among the top 10 topics in both dataset, we find many of similar topics and sub-topics that were uncovered earlier (Bruns et al., 2022;Lindelöf et al., 2022;Zhao et al., 2023). The high ranking of topics like Sputnik, EU vaccine, and Israel, appears to be unique in the Finnish context.

Further, nearly all discernible topics from both datasets can be categorized under concerns about vaccine safety and efficacy, echoing Skafle et al.'s (2022), findings that fear of side effects is a prominent theme in vaccine hesitancy. They suggest that these fears can evolve into misinformation and hyperbolic portrayal of side effects. Accordingly, a primary objective of crisis communication should be to assuage these fears.

Additionally, the pandemic has highlighted the public's quest for scientific evidence. Pulido et al. (2020) revealed that despite the higher frequency of tweeting misinformation, science-based or fact-checking tweets were retweeted more often, underscoring the significance of circulating credible information. Indeed, the participation of medical experts in public discussions, together with health authorities, serves as an effective strategy to address these concerns (Malecki et al., 2021). This approach becomes even more impactful when it involves the correction of misinformation (Walter et al., 2021). This approach not only allows for the swift dissemination of information but also fosters trust and builds relationships with the public.

Previous studies have demonstrated how fears and concerns about vaccine-related uncertainties are exploited to target the erosion and undermining of public trust in government institutions, organizations, and mainstream media (Kou et al., 2017;Lindelöf et al., 2022). Certain studies have highlighted the involvement of foreign entities in these campaigns, which warrants further scrutiny (Bernard et al., 2021). Mistrust is particularly evident among individuals exposed to misinformation. Thus, the proliferation of misinformation surrounding COVID-19 can significantly impact the ways in which people seek and trust information (Bruns et al., 2022). For instance, such claims may include allegations of corruption as the primary or sole explanation for actions taken by authorities. They may also suggest that large pharmaceutical corporations have hired scientists to engineer the virus, that scientists have received bribes from big corporations, or that the media is either exaggerating the severity of the virus or downplaying its side effects.

Effective public health communication is paramount in curbing the spread of misinformation and NSV. This involves not only the timely dissemination of relevant pandemic information, but also equipping individuals with the skills to comprehend and interpret the deluge of information online. Prebunking interventions, which help individuals recognize misinformation and understand associated tactics, play a crucial role (Bruns et al., 2022). For example, nudges, interventions that subtly influence behavior without limiting choices or altering incentives, can foster critical thinking (Bruns et al., 2022). By educating the public to scrutinize information sources, skepticism towards unreliable sources, such as anonymous accounts often associated with misinformation and NSV, can be increased (Cinelli et al., 2020;De Magistris et al., 2022;K. Zhang & Kizilcec, 2014). Likewise, instructing the public to consider accuracy can effectively guide their decisions about what information to circulate on social media (Pennycook et al., 2020).

Conversely, as highlighted in previous studies (Bennett, 2023;Gagliardone et al., 2021), there was an evident void of an authoritative figure actively countering such misinformation, even though a minority of accounts disseminated a vast quantity of it. These groups often spread misinformation due to predispositions towards conspiracy theories and the cognitive mechanism of confirmation bias. They also bond over shared ideologies, creating an echo chamber effect that amplifies misinformation (Bruns et al., 2022;Rathje et al., 2022). Moreover, algorithms tend to promote and support user engagements with polarized and inflammatory content, as these engagements generate revenue for companies (Bennett, 2023).

Overall, general sentiment towards authorities (i.e., THL), perceptions of COVID-19 vaccination, and actual vaccine uptake in Finland all remain high. Furthermore, in addition to the involvement of PHAs, the participation of experts as individuals is crucial. Our results indicate that public institutions may be targeted by malicious bots or trolls aiming to obstruct access to reliable information. In such scenarios, experts can play a pivotal role by disseminating accurate information and debunking false claims across various channels within the same platform.

Finally, it is crucial to recognize that misinformation and NSV represent only a fraction of the broader discourse on COVID-19. The majority of conversations contain content unrelated to misinformation or NSV, highlighting that the larger part of the online audience engages in discussions that could potentially offer positive insights and information. This underscores the importance of acknowledging that social media, despite its challenges, also serves as a platform for beneficial dialogue and exchange of helpful information.

Our study reveals that a substantial proportion of tweets circulating misinformation and NSV during the pandemic; roughly one in three tweets propagated false, deceitful, malicious information about vaccinations. User engagement, including shares and likes, with these problematic tweets did not differ significantly (Kouzy et al., 2020). This suggests that misinformation can easily disseminate on social media platforms, regardless of source reliability (Cinelli et al., 2020). Lindelöf et al. (2022) found that the number of NSV and conspiracy theories declined following the initial global English Twitter discourse on vaccine rollout. However, our findings suggest that, after this initial reduction, a significant spike was observed during the period of the third vaccine rollout and the discovery of the COVID-19 Omicron variant.

Our research uncovered a strong correlation between the spread of misinformation and NSV on Twitter, noting a particularly alarming rise in both factors throughout the pandemic. This linear relationship aligns with findings from Skafle et al. (2022) and is further evidenced by Loomba et al. (2021) in their randomized controlled trial, who found that online misinformation led to a decrease in vaccination intent by 6.2% in the UK and 6.4% in the USA. Pierri et al. (2022) also identified a negative correlation between misinformation and COVID-19 vaccination uptake rates, reinforcing the link between misinformation and NSV. Interestingly, the situation in Finland diverges from these trends. Despite the prevalence of NSV in online discussions, Finland's vaccination rates have remained high, suggesting that the vocal online NSV community does not reflect the wider population's views. This observation points towards the complexity of translating social media sentiment into actual behavioral outcomes, with Finland exemplifying how real-world actions can deviate from online discourse.

Our analysis indicates temporal fluctuations in the spread of misinformation and NSV, with periods of peak activity followed by stabilization. This pattern may suggest a saturation or normalization of these narratives within public discourse.

However, the challenge of drawing concrete conclusions from social media data cannot be understated, given the complexities of research design and data limitations. The dynamic nature of social media complicates the identification of clear causal relationships, necessitating a cautious approach to data interpretation and emphasizing the importance of rigorous methodology in understanding the impact of misinformation on public health decisions.

The role of bots in this discourse is noteworthy, especially given the increase of malicious bots over the course of the pandemic (Al-Rawi & Shukla, 2020;Suarez-Lledo & Alvarez-Galvez, 2022;Weng & Lin, 2022). Our study identified a significant number of bot accounts actively involved in disseminating misinformation and expressing NSV concerning COVID-19. However, these accounts were not the primary influencers in shaping the overall discourse on Twitter. Our independent bot analysis (Unlu et al., n.d.) reveals that the range of topics propagated by malicious bots is broad and varies in response to the pandemic's progression, mirroring overall trends. The key focus of these bots' messages revolves around safety, political/conspiracy theories, and choicerelated issues. They exhibit a pattern of critiquing pandemic containment strategies, expressing dissent towards political figures, and sowing skepticism about the veracity of information disseminated on social media.

Through BERTopic modeling, we uncovered the complex and evolving nature of vaccine discourse on Twitter during the COVID-19 pandemic. We observed a significant upsurge in NSV and misinformation discussions in the end of 2021, gradually tapering thereafter. This trend reflects the fluidity of public sentiment in response to evolving circumstances and information during a health crisis.

The discourse originally focused on the purported ties between Bill Gates and pharmaceutical company GSK, shifting later towards vaccine hesitancy. This trend began in late 2020 and peaked in December 2021, with notable keywords being prevention, death, and Pfizer, suggesting public sentiment was swayed by perceptions of the vaccine's safety and efficacy. Our findings suggest that groups propelled by conspiracy theories consistently introduced new agendas to public discourse, contributing to the persistence of such theories. In this stage, vaccinerelated misinformation was disseminated not only by anti-vaccine activists but also systematically by professional news sources across several countries (Jemielniak & Krempovych, 2021). However, while political ideology and reliance on conservative media can exacerbate the situation, vaccination intentions in the US context were discovered to have less correlated with political ideology (Romer & Jamieson, 2020). Furthermore, given that Finland has one of the highest levels of trust in news media and press readership in the EU (Ala-Fossi et al., 2023), incorporating mainstream media appears to be effective in promoting both preventive actions and vaccination uptake.

Our model also revealed that the key topics in tweets promoting misinformation mirrored those causing vaccine hesitancy, such as the death of vaccinated individuals, prevention of COVID infection, and vaccine effectiveness. This suggests that those spreading misinformation and those hesitant to vaccinate share similar concerns.

Through exploring the common themes among the top 10 topics in both dataset, we find many of similar topics and sub-topics that were uncovered earlier (Bruns et al., 2022;Lindelöf et al., 2022;Zhao et al., 2023). The high ranking of topics like Sputnik, EU vaccine, and Israel, appears to be unique in the Finnish context.

sections.paper_id: Unlu et al. - 2025 - Tracing the dynamics of misinformation and vaccine stance in Finland amid COVID-19
paragraphs: This study has several limitations that may affect the generalizability and validity of the findings. It neglects potential influences like government policies and personal beliefs and relies on a non-representative sample of social media users. Self-reported data could lead to bias, and the correlational data cannot confirm causation.

The study's focus on Finland and its use of cross-sectional social media data from Twitter, which may have representational limitations, could restrict the generalizability of its findings. Furthermore, Twitter data, shown to contain the most health misinformation (Suarez-Lledo & Alvarez-Galvez, 2022), does not represent broader public opinion and is subject to biases, manipulation, and accessibility issues, which could impact research accuracy.

sections.paper_id: Unlu et al. - 2025 - Tracing the dynamics of misinformation and vaccine stance in Finland amid COVID-19
paragraphs: Our study illuminates the dynamics of vaccine-related discourse on Twitter, underscoring how misinformation on Twitter impacts NSV on Twitter. These insights carry significant weight for public health policy and communication strategies, emphasizing the need for accurate information dissemination and misinformation mitigation to bolster vaccine uptake.

The distinct correlation between misinformation and NSV reveals the urgent need for public health initiatives to address this issue. Conversely, one could argue that the initiatives and actions taken in Finland have been effective, as evidenced by the high levels of acceptance and vaccine uptake. This might involve promoting accurate information and quelling misinformation. Additionally, the role of malicious bots in propagating misinformation suggests that policy measures might need to manage their usage on social media platforms.

By implementing responsive, tailored communication strategies that address public concerns, health authorities can effectively combat misinformation and reduce vaccine hesitancy. This active engagement with the ongoing discourse can strengthen public trust in vaccines, encourage broader acceptance, and expedite our progress towards the pandemic's end.

paragraphs: Ali Unlu, PhD, is a Senior Researcher specializing in public policy. He has a broad interdisciplinary focus, with areas of expertise encompassing drug policy, crisis management, and hate speech. He leverages the power of artificial intelligence tools and methodologies in his work. Dr. Unlu conducts comprehensive research on social media platforms to gain insights into public opinion on these pivotal topics.
Sophie Truong is a master's student with a background in web development, machine learning, and Machine Learning Operations (MLOps).

Nitin Sawhney is a Professor of Practice in the Department of Computer Science at Aalto University. Working at the intersection of human computer interaction, participatory design research, and ethical AI he examines the critical role of technology, civic agency, and justice in society and crisis contexts.

Jonas Sivelä, PhD is the Head of the Cultural, Behavioural, and Media Insights Centre (CUBE) at the Finnish Institute for Health and Welfare. He is responsible for activities and research related to vaccine hesitancy at the institute. Alongside his research responsibilities, he also has an extensive professional background in communications and journalism.

Tuukka Tammi, PhD (sociology) is a Research Programme Director at the Finnish Institute for Health and Welfare (THL) in Helsinki, Finland. He has published many studies on drug policy, harm reduction, drug treatment as well as gambling and other addictions. Currently, he is leading THL's part of the research projects 'Reconstructing Crisis Narratives for Trustworthy Communication and Cooperative Agency,' funded by the Academy of Finland, and 'DRUG-PREP -Strengthening preparedness of drug information systems and drug policy capacities to respond to emerging drug trends and challenges,' funded by the European Commission (DG JUST).
